bigQuestion: Where are we in the universe?

examples: I will visit those places again, Bella thought.
Scientists will find new solar systems in our galaxy.
Scientists will send a person to Mars someday.
She will become an astronaut when she grows up.
rules: Use will to make a prediction.
A prediction is something you believe about the future.
A prediction is not a fact.

bigQuestion: Where are we in the universe?

examples: If technology continues to grow, we will travel to these places ourselves.
We will travel to these places ourselves if technology continues to grow.
If I become an astronaut, I will walk on the surface of Mars.
If I study hard, I will get good grades.
rules: Use the future real conditional to talk about a future possibility and what will happen as a result of it.
Use the word if with a future possibility.
The if clause can come first or second in the sentence.

bigQuestion: How do we know what happened long ago?

examples: The Chinese government plans to keep it closed for now.
All the students promised to find more terracotta soldiers.
The archaeologists tried to build the clay army.
I forgot to pack my lunch.
rules: Some verbs are often followed by an infinitive.
An infinitive is a verb with the word to in front of it.
Common verbs followed by infinitives include: plan, promise, try, forget, want, decide, need.

bigQuestion: How do we know what happened long ago?

examples: Javier enjoyed playing with his friends.
When it began raining, the excavation team went inside.
The museum staff finished cleaning the dinosaur bones.
I enjoy swimming in a pool on a hot day.
rules: Gerunds are verbs that end in -ing.
Gerunds can act like nouns.
Verbs like enjoy, finish, and practice are often followed by gerunds.

bigQuestion: Where does our food come from?

examples: I'm making a special breakfast today.
We're playing soccer after school today.
My parents are having dinner at a French restaurant tomorrow.
I'm cleaning my bedroom on Saturday morning.
rules: The present continuous tense can tell about future actions that someone plans.
Include the future time when you use this tense.
Use am/is/are + verb-ing to form the present continuous.

topic: Food and Farming: The World Around Us
bigQuestion: Where does our food come from?

examples: Would you like to know where your food comes from?
Would you like a delicious apple from South Africa?
Would you like to bake a cake with me?
Would you like a piece of apple pie?
rules: Use Would you like ... ? to make a polite offer.
You can use this phrase to offer an action or a thing.
Would you like + to + verb (action) or Would you like + noun (thing).

examples: Can anyone make art?
I want to paint something in this room.
Everyone worked on the sculpture.
There is something I like about this sculpture.
rules: Use indefinite pronouns to talk about unnamed people or things.
Anyone means any of the people.
Something means one of the things.
Everyone means all of the people.

examples: "Shall I show you some more sketches?" he asks.
"I will sign you up for something," his mother offers.
Shall I bring you a cold drink?
I'll carry that for you.
rules: Use shall to make an offer with a question.
Use will to make an offer with a statement.
Shall I ...? is used to offer to do something for someone.

examples: Do you have nothing to do?
No one could see everything.
Nobody is in the museum.
There is nothing in my pocket.
rules: Indefinite pronouns refer to people or objects that we don't name or know.
Negative indefinite pronouns begin with no-.
Nothing means not anything; no one and nobody mean no person.

examples: You'll work hard, won't you?
Marcy wasn't nervous, was she?
Ann is the youngest student, isn't she?
You're ready for the test, aren't you?
rules: In a tag question, a statement is turned into a question by adding a tag at the end.
A tag question always has a yes or no answer.
Tag questions are often used to say things that we think are true, but that we don't know for sure.

examples: Max Axiom studies these fascinating, tiny visitors.
The doctor washed his white, new lab coat.
Max saw round, red, large viruses.
There is a long, white, plastic ruler on my desk.
rules: You can use more than one adjective to describe a noun.
The adjectives must go in a certain order: opinion ... size ... age ... shape ... color ... kind.
Opinion adjectives come before size adjectives.

examples: In ancient times, people used to think differently about the human body.
When I was young, I used to ride my bike.
My sister used to go to the swimming pool often as a child.
I used to take the bus to school.
rules: Use used to before an action that happened often in the past, but not now.
Used to describes a past habit or state that no longer exists.
Compare with simple past: simple past describes one completed action; used to describes repeated past actions.

grammar.point: Must, Mustn't, Have To, Don't Have To
examples: You must pay for the Internet to use it at home.
You mustn't take a newspaper without paying for it.
You don't have to carry a computer to check the news.
You must walk quietly in the halls.
rules: Use must and have to to talk about what a person needs to do.
Use mustn't to talk about what a person shouldn't do.
Use don't have to to talk about what a person doesn't need to do.

examples: I have decided to keep a blog.
He hasn't written a blog before.
The reporter has done many interviews.
I haven't met a reporter before.
rules: Use the present perfect tense to tell what happened in the past, but did not happen at a specific time.
Use the past participle form of the main verb with has or have.
Use hasn't / haven't for negative sentences.

bigQuestion: What can we learn from nature's power?

grammar.point: Present Perfect with Ever and Never
examples: Have you ever seen a real tornado?
Yes, I have. / No, I haven't. I've never seen a real tornado.
Have you ever experienced an earthquake?
I've never felt an earthquake.
rules: Use the present perfect with ever and never to ask and answer questions about life experiences.
Use ever in questions to ask about any time up to now.
Use never in statements to say something has not happened at any time.

bigQuestion: What can we learn from nature's power?

grammar.point: Present Perfect with Already, Just, and Yet
examples: I've already packed everything.
I've just eaten dinner.
Have you cleaned your room yet? No, I haven't cleaned it yet.
I've already made twelve sandwiches and four pizzas.
rules: You can use the present perfect with the adverbs already, just, and yet.
These adverbs tell when something happened or didn't happen.
Already and just are used in positive statements; yet is used in questions and negative statements.

grammar.point: Present Perfect with For and Since
examples: These biomes have provided a home for animals for a long time.
I have studied biomes since February.
My dad has studied mammals for twenty years.
The government has protected this forest since 1998.
rules: Use the present perfect with for and since to show that something began in the past and is still happening now.
Use for to talk about an amount of time.
Use since to talk about a specific point of time in the past.

examples: Camila has created a petition.
When she got home, Camila created a petition.
I have run in a race. I ran in a race last summer.
They have snorkeled this morning.
rules: Use the present perfect for past experiences, past actions that continue to the present, or actions that happened at an unspecific time.
Use the simple past for completed actions that happened at a specific time.
The simple past is used when we know when something happened.
